behaviors.description: Renamed files appear in their own category, not counted as deletion plus addition
behaviors.justification: The failure is in parsing/categorization logic. Mocking git's 'git diff --name-status' output with 'R100 old.py new.py' lets us verify the code correctly identifies the R status and counts it as 1 rename (not 1 delete + 1 add). The subprocess and git behavior are not the failure point; the parsing logic is.
behaviors.test_description: Mock git diff --name-status to return rename entries (R status). Assert that the parse_diff() function categorizes them under 'renamed' (count=1) rather than splitting into 'deleted' and 'added'. Test edge cases: R50 (low similarity), unicode filenames.

behaviors.description: Binary files in diffs do not crash tool; binary files are counted in output
behaviors.justification: Mocking the 'Binary files differ' marker might pass, but real subprocess output handling and character encoding are the real risks. UTF-8 decode errors, output buffer handling, and real git's actual formatting of binary file entries can't be tested with mocks. A real git repo with an actual binary file (PNG, ZIP, etc.) catches subprocess I/O edge cases that mocks hide.
behaviors.test_description: Create test git repo. Commit an actual binary file (.png, .exe, or similar). Run the tool. Assert: (1) no crash/exception, (2) binary file is counted in the output, (3) tool completes successfully. Verify with both '-staged' and '-unstaged' modes if applicable.

behaviors.description: Tool produces empty report on repository with no commits, not an error
behaviors.justification: This is error-handling logic. We can mock git to raise the error that occurs with an empty repo (e.g., 'fatal: your current branch does not have any commits yet') and verify the code catches it and returns empty report. The error condition itself can be simulated; no real subprocess or git behavior is needed to test the error path.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the git subprocess call to raise a 'no commits' error (or return status code indicating no history). Assert that the tool catches the exception and returns an empty report structure (empty lists for added, deleted, renamed, modified). Verify no exception propagates to caller.

behaviors.description: Counts in report match actual change history of repository
behaviors.justification: This is a ground-truth correctness test. Mocking would just validate 'does our code match our mock?' — not 'is our code correct?'. We need a real git repository with a known sequence of commits to validate against ground truth. Create: 3 added files, 2 deleted files, 1 renamed file, 4 modified files, then verify tool reports exactly these counts.
behaviors.test_description: Create a test git repository with known commits: (1) initial commit with 5 files, (2) add 3 new files, (3) delete 2 files, (4) rename 1 file with 'git mv', (5) modify 4 files. Run tool on this repo. Assert: counts exactly match (added=3, deleted=2, renamed=1, modified=4). Verify across multiple commit ranges and branch scenarios.

behaviors.description: Output identical whether changes analyzed are staged or unstaged
behaviors.justification: Staging status is a real git state that can only be tested with an actual repository. Mocks can't represent the actual index/working-directory separation. The tool must call the correct git commands to capture both staged and unstaged changes. We need a real repo with staged files (git add) and unstaged changes (edits not added) to verify the output is consistent regardless of staging status.
behaviors.test_description: Create test git repo. Make changes: stage some files (git add), leave others unstaged (modified but not added). Run tool. Capture output. Verify output includes all changed files consistently. Optionally: reset staging and verify output remains identical. Test both 'staged only' and 'all changes' interpretation to ensure tool spec is met.
